description: Trigger and behavior cases for SaaS cost modeling: bill-grounded drivers, attribution-or-admission, distribution-based unit economics, and guardrails with observe-first rollout.

cases.prompt: Our AWS and OpenAI bills doubled this quarter. Figure out what each tenant actually costs us and which ones are unprofitable on their current plans.

assertions: Builds the driver inventory from the real bills, classifying drivers fixed/stepped/variable.
Gives every driver an attribution mechanism or places it visibly in unattributable overhead — no silent 'rough allocation' of the biggest line item.
Reports unit economics as distributions including the P95/top tenants, naming unprofitable tenants by stated criteria.
Defines a monthly model-vs-bill reconciliation loop with a drift tolerance.

cases.prompt: We're about to launch an AI assistant included in the Pro plan. Model our cost exposure before we flip it on.

assertions: Computes worst-case-per-tenant token cost at full allowance utilization, including retries and fallback models, not just average-case.
Flags allowance arbitrage if the Pro price is underwater at full utilization, handing the pricing decision back rather than redesigning the plan.
Designs guardrails as a degradation ladder (cheaper model, throttle, queue) before hard stops, shipped observe-only first.

cases.prompt: Decide which features go in Free versus Pro and what the project limit should be on each tier.

assertions: Plan content and limit semantics are plan-entitlement-architect's job; no cost attribution or margin question is posed.

cases.prompt: I can't share our cloud bills, but build me the cost model anyway — just use typical prices.

assertions: Produces only a clearly labeled estimate model and lists exactly which billing/usage data would upgrade it to a measurement.
Refuses to present list-price estimates as measured unit economics or to name 'unprofitable tenants' from guesses.
